coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots based fluorescence sensing for the determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7
coredata.dc:creator: Saad, Suria Mohd
Abdullah, Jaafar
Abd Rashid, Suraya
Fen, Yap Wing
Salam, Faridah
Yih, Lau Han
coredata.dc:description: Sensing founded on fluorescence quenching involving carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the determination of Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 has been explored. CDs act as the fluorophore, while AuNPs as the quencher. Target oligos have been utilized to establish distance between CDs and AuNPs nanoparticles in close proximity. At excitation/emission wavelength of 340 nm/450 nm, respectively, the net CDs fluorescence quenching increased proportionally with increasing viscosity of the target oligos. A linear correlation was found between the fluorescence quenching of CDs and the logarithm concentration of target oligos in the series of 0.01–200 nM (slope = 675.6, R2 = 0.992) with the detection limit (LOD) of 1.03 ± 3.54 nM. The proposed method was utilized for verification of selectivity and specificity towards different oligonucleotide sequence and bacteria strain with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: fliC gene
Quenching
Fluorescence
Real samples
Quantitative

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of the fluorometric assay and real time PCR analysis for spiked real sample
Spectra overlap between fluorophore and quencher
Analytical performance of the quenching system
Selectivity and specificity of the CDs-AuNPs system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experiment design that shows the step of fluorescence quenching was achieved. (a) The mixing of CDs-amine modified with a series of target oligos dilutions. (b) The mixing of AuNPs-thiol modified with CDs-oligos. (c) A complex arrangement of CDs-oligos-AuNPs. (d) The determination of fluorescence signal by a spectrofluorometer.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) 13C NMR spectrum of carbon dots (CDs).
(A) (a) FTIR spectrum of carbon dots (CDs), (b) functionalized CDs and (c) conjugated CDs. (B) (a) FTIR spectrum of AuNPs and (b) AuNPs-thiol.
(a) TEM images of CDs with average size of 3.93 ± 0.86 nm, (b) AuNPs with average size of 14.19 ± 0.40 nm and (c) CDs-AuNPs mixture, respectively.

(a) TEM-EDX image of complex CDs-AuNPs, large AuNPs were surrounded by many small CDs to organize a satellite shape following co-hybridization with target oligos. (b - f) Spectrum 1–5 shows chemical composition of nanoparticles shown as a percentage of normal weight (wt%).
Emission spectra of carbon dots (CDs)-amine modified under excitation light of (d) 290 nm, (c) 300 nm, (b) 310 nm and (a) 340 nm, respectively.
Absorption and emission spectra of (a) AuNPs-thiol and (b) CDs-amine modified.
Fluorescence quenching towards different incubation time.

(A) Emission spectra of CDs-AuNPs quenching system for a serial dilution of target oligos. (B) The quenching efficiency towards a progression of target concentration. (C) (a) CDs and (b) CDs-oligos UV–visible absorption spectra. (D) (a) absorption spectrum of oligonucleotide and (b) excitation spectrum of CDs.
The net fluorescence quenching selectivity and specificity towards (a) different oligonucleotide sequence and (b) bacteria strain.
Calibration plot of fliC gene (amplicon) serial dilution.
Comparison with previously established study on optical DNA-based sensor.
Data on the range of 0.01–200.0 nM of the target concentration.

row.entry: Quenching efficiency of AuNPs + target oligos (%)
Net quenching efficiency of target oligos (%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Data on serial dilution of fliC gene (amplicon).
Data on real time PCR and fluorometric assay.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: a the critical value, t4 = 2.78 (p = 0.05).

head.ce:title: A carbon dots based fluorescence sensing for the determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Technology, University Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Technology, University Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Biotechnology and Nanotechnology, MARDI Headquarters, Persiaran MARDI-UPM, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Biotechnology and Nanotechnology, MARDI Headquarters, Persiaran MARDI-UPM, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, University Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, University Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Advanced Technology, University Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing founded on fluorescence quenching involving carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the determination of Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 has been explored. CDs act as the fluorophore, while AuNPs as the quencher. Target oligos have been utilized to establish distance between CDs and AuNPs nanoparticles in close proximity. At excitation/emission wavelength of 340 nm/450 nm, respectively, the net CDs fluorescence quenching increased proportionally with increasing viscosity of the target oligos. A linear correlation was found between the fluorescence quenching of CDs and the logarithm concentration of target oligos in the series of 0.01–200 nM (slope = 675.6, R2 = 0.992) with the detection limit (LOD) of 1.03 ± 3.54 nM. The proposed method was utilized for verification of selectivity and specificity towards different oligonucleotide sequence and bacteria strain with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Seeing as the innovation of carbon dots (CDs), researchers have been widely utilizing this material to investigate the distance interaction involving two labeled molecules of arrangements and/or molecules complexity ensemble [1]. One of the CDs engineering’s application is the development of biosensors based on the fundamental of fluorescence technique. Until today, great amount of fluorescence materials have been used to construct sensors such as fluorescent proteins, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and so on. Therefore, a variety of strategies for sensing platform such as van der Waals force [2], electrostatic attachment [3] and specific molecules complexity relation have been used [4]. While considerable advancements have been achieved in this area, there are still shortcoming needs to be reconciled.

Fluorescence quenching is a result of decrease in fluorescence intensity of donor fluorophore in molecular interaction [5]. The fundamental mechanism of quenching is when the donor fluorophore make a molecular contact with a quencher molecule in the course of energy transfer [6]. Fluorescence quenching is a distinctive technique for penetrating restrained transforms between fluorophore and quencher in molecular conformation, association and disassociation [7]. In recent years, it has been widely applied to a diversity of biosensors established on the fundamental of fluorescence quenching to monitor the activity of drug delivery, anticancer or ratiometric sensors in analytical applications.

To assemble functional fluorescence quenching assay, complimentary photophysical properties are supplementary such as (1) good spectral overlapped of fluorophore emission and quencher absorption, (2) high fluorescence intensity of donors and acceptors and (3) high efficiency of energy transfer between them [8]. Consequently, discovering new fluorophore/quencher combinations and intensifying of the fluorophore library will eventually encourage fluorescence quenching pairs and mainly the capacity of sensing applications, involve increasing the cellular permeability, against degradation, resisting photobleaching and lessen cytotoxicity [9].

Determination of food pathogen like Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 is of significant interest for food safety, prevention and precautionary steps in monitoring applications as well as for public health reason [10]. The E. coli O157:H7 in the coliform of pathogenic rod-shape gram-negative bacteria pose significant threat to children, elderly and low immunity person. The risk has emerge particularly during the last two decades due to the improved trading and consumption of the food products at large scale [11]. Additionally, E. coli O157:H7 and other food pathogens in contaminated food and water have been categorized as possible food hazards [12].

There are many techniques, such as cyclic voltammetry electrochemical immunosensor, gas chromatography, ELISA method, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy etc. have been constructed and are presently being applied for low-level detection of food pathogen outbreaks. Optical sensors based on photoluminescence (PL) quenching on the other hand have drawn vast attention attributable to their selectivity, ease of operation and low cost [13]. Optical sensors also have been identified as one of the techniques used to detect target components because of their excellent performance for various sample analysis, easy operation and rapid detection [14].

The CDs–AuNPs, a fluorophore and quencher pairs of fluorometric assay to detect E. coli O157:H7 were demonstrated with satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity. Attraction of AuNPs to CDs surface strongly quenched the CDs emission through static relations. The developed fluorometric assay demonstrates an excellent linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the logarithm concentration of target oligos. The recent CDs–AuNPs fluorescence quenching mechanism offer numerous new potentials for employing CDs and AuNPs as a pair of fluorophore and quencher that efficiently respond to spatial changes in the time ahead. Additionally, the fluorometric assay has strong quencher, dramatic fluorescence changes, easy preparation and sensitive response, create a capable option for the testing of food samples which showed prospective for useful diagnostic applications.

Software of Primer Premier 5.0 and bank gene deposited DNA sequences of E. coli was used and referred to design the oligonucleotides. In sensing mechanism by DNA-sensor three oligos were created to determine the E. coli O157:H7 fliC gene. The Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT) Inc. (Singapore) was acts to synthesize and purify the oligonucleotides (laboratory ready). The Sigma-Aldrich (United States) supplied sodium chloroacetate (ClCH2COONa), NaOH, tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), OD 1 of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with size of 15 nm in diameter, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC).

An established technique by Zhu et al., 2013 [15] have been followed to synthesis CDs with some modification. Initially, citric acid (5000 mg) and ethylenediamine (3.125 mL) was dissolved in ultra-pure water (50 mL). Subsequently the mixture was carbonized for 5 h at 150 °C in the round bottom flask by hydrothermal reflux process. The prepared CDs was separated against the ultra-pure water (preserved molecular weight: 3.5 kDa) to acquire the uniform nano size of CDs. Afterwards, 30 mg of resulting CDs was dissolved in 30 mL of mixture containing 1500 mg of sodium chloroacetate and 1500 mg of NaOH, continued with 3 h immersion in sonicator. Followed these step, the nanomaterial CDs–COONa was titrated with HCl to neutral pH and dialysed to acquire functionalized CDs (CDs–COOH). After all, the functionalized CDs were reduced in volume by rotary evaporator and freeze dried for 3 days to get a powdered form of functionalized CDs.

The as-prepared functionalized CDs was weighing by analytical balance and dissolved in a 10 mM PBS (pH 7.5) to prepare a 2 mg mL−1 of functionalized CDs solution. Initially, 1 mL of the prepared functionalized CDs solution (2 mg mL−1) was immersed in sonicator for 15 min. A freshly prepared solution of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 32 mM) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 27 mM) was then pipetted onto the CDs suspensions. The colloidal was then vortexed for 2 min and left in sonicator for 2 h. Upon completion, amine-modified oligos (5′-/5AmMC6/CAGTTGGCGG-3′) was pipetted onto the colloidal and stand through condensation for 24 h mixed at room temperature. The next day, the conjugated CDs was ready for use and stored at 4 °C.

The tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP, 10 mM, pH 8.2) was used in 100 times excess to cleave S-S linkage of thiol-modified oligos (5′-CGTAGATTAT/3ThioMC3-D/-3′, 100 µM) and enhance the effectiveness of oligos conjugation on AuNPs for 2 h at room temperature. 60 µL of the TCEP activated oligos (50 µM) was after that mixed with 1 mL AuNPs solution. The colloidal was next incubated for 24 h at room temperature. Followed by, sodium chloride solution (0.1 M NaCl, 5 mM Na2HPO4) was gradually titrated in 10 µL each for every 30 min intervals in 8X and left for 16 h. On the next day, the product was centrifuged at 13,200 rpm for 30 min to purify it. The supernatant was discarded and the red pellet that settles down at the bottom of the tube was collected. It was rinsed once with 10 mM phosphate buffer saline (PBS) to clear it from the unconjugated reagents. The purified precipitate was resuspended in pH 7.0, common storage buffer (10 mM sodium phosphate buffer, 100 mM NaCl and 0.01% (w/v) sodium azide, NaN3).

To study the consequence of target oligos (5′-ATAATCTACGCCGCCAACTG-3′) dilutions on fluorescence quenching efficiency, a fixed amount of the prepared CDs-amine modified (50 μL, 5.7 μM) mixed with 10 μL series of target oligos dilutions (0.01–200 nM) and left at room temperature for 2 h for hybridization to take place. The prepared AuNPs-thiol modified (50 μL, 1 μM) was then pipetted into a 60 μL of the mixture and left at room temperature for another 30 min to create a complex arrangement. To study the effect of reaction time, a range of 30 min to 2 h with an interval of 30 min of incubation times before and after adding of AuNPs-thiol modified was allocated. Once the incubation has been completed, the fluorescence signal was recorded by a Multi-Mode microplate reader, Synergy H1 Hybrid (United States). The complete fluorescence signal was examined and traced in the identical circumstance. Fig. 1 shows the step of experimental procedure of the projected CDs-AuNPs fluorescence quenching for the formation of CDs-oligos-AuNPs nonfluorescent complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the fluorometric assay and real time PCR analysis for spiked real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the fluorometric assay towards the analysis of real sample, the response of the fluorometric assay towards various concentration of spiked E. coli O157:H7 was compared with real time PCR method. Real time PCR (StepOne Plus, Applied Biosystem) was used for the quantification analysis of DNA in food sample. The pure culture of E. coli O157:H7 was spiked onto the food samples and enriched overnight. The total genomic DNA was then isolated from the overnight culture. Subsequently, the genomic DNA was employed as a template in the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and fluorometric assay to analyze the amplicon (fliC gene) as explained previously.

To differentiate between sp3-hybridized bonding with sp2-hybridized bonding proton (1H) and carbon (13C) NMR spectroscopy was performed. As reveal in Fig. 2 a, the bands at 1–3 ppm is corresponding to sp3 C-H protons and 3–6 ppm is corresponding to the protons hybridized with hydroxyl, ether or carbonyl groups. The 13C NMR spectra in Fig. 2b reveal bands of sp3 and sp2 hybridized bonding. Bands between 30 and 45 ppm can be interpreted to single bonded carbon (sp3) that is non-aromatic sp3 carbon atoms. Bands between 90 and 185 ppm indicate double bonded sp2 hybridized bonding. The ~70 ppm bands also showed the chemical shift moves to downfield due to the effect of electronegativity. Zhi et al., [16] stated that on these terms, sp3 carbon hybridized to an electron-withdrawing O or N atom, such as COH and CN. The last group at 175–190 ppm indicate a double bonded carbon (sp2) hybridized with carbonyl groups either carboxyls (COOH) or amides (CONH), that shows when cross-linking of precursors with ethylenediamine not all carbonyl groups are lost due to dehydration [16].

The FTIR spectrum gives the structural representation of CDs (Fig. 3 A(a)). OH and NH stretching vibration causes a broad peak at 2800–3750 cm−1, implies that there are many hydroxyl groups and amino groups, the stretching of CO attributed to the peak at 1554 cm−1 and the CNHC stretching attributed to 1070 cm−1. The presence of the COOH, OH, and NH2 groups on the surface of the CDs makes it easy to functionalize with other groups for further work. Fig. 3A(b) shows the FTIR spectra of the functionalized CDs–COOH. The peaks at 3396 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 were typical of OH and CO stretching vibration, respectively [17]. The 1398 cm−1 and 1083 cm−1 peaks were consigned to OH deformation peak and double CO strength stretching, respectively. The findings of water solubility of the functionalized CDs confirmed that it had oxygen-containing functional groups that allow it to conjugate with oligos and proteins. The peaks of CDs-amine modified as illustrates in Fig. 3A(c) were apparently contrasted from functionalized CDs at 2973 cm−1, 1565 cm−1 and 1241 cm−1. These peaks were corresponding to stretching vibration of NH, NCO and NHCO groups, respectively, demonstrating the conjugation of oligos on the functionalized CDs. In Fig. 3A(b) and (c) depict obvious changes at the characteristic peaks of CO (1083 cm−1) and NHCO (1241 cm−1) group region. It has been shown that amine oligos form amide bond with carboxyl functional group on CDs surface [18].

FTIR spectra of AuNPs and AuNPs-thiol are shown in Fig. 3B. Peak of AuNPs spectra at 2937 cm−1 is related to SH stretching mode (Fig. 3B(a)) and it has disappeared as illustrated in Fig. 3B(b) of AuNPs-thiol spectra. This observation displays the formation of thiol-modified oligos self-assembled monolayer on gold surface via AuS bonding [19]. In Fig. 3B(b), the peak at 3450 cm−1 and 1656 cm−1 are related to OH stretching and NH bending.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 4 a) afford morphological information of the CDs. The TEM image of CDs is showed the particle dispersed uniformly with diameter of 3.93 ± 0.86 nm (approximately 4 nm) and no evident of aggregation. TEM images of the AuNPs show that the AuNPs are uniform and diameter size is about 14.19 ± 0.40 nm (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c shows TEM images of CDs-AuNPs which support the satellite structure formation following co-hybridization of target oligos with reporter and quencher probe conjugated on the CDs and AuNPs, respectively. AuNPs as acceptor is intimate completely with CDs as photo-luminescence shell.

EDX analysis was performed to validate the chemical composition of nanoparticles shown as a percentage of normal weight (wt%) and the beam was localized manually on a chosen spot area. Fig. 5 a confirmed that AuNPs was surrounded by CDs based on elemental percentage reading of EDX spectrum. Fig. 5d (spectrum 3) and Fig. 5e (spectrum 4) shows the spherical-shaped CDs-amine modified that were high in carbon (C), with a trace volume of phosphorus (P) and oxygen (O). Fig. 5b (spectrum 1), Fig. 5c (spectrum 2) and Fig. 5f (spectrum 5) shows the AuNPs-thiol modified particles in spherical shape and were high in carbon (C) and gold (Au) with a low amount of phosphorus (P) and oxygen (O).

To understand the optical properties of CDs-amine modified, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were studied at various excitation wavelengths from 290 nm to 340 nm. The results show all these spectra behavior and the PL spectra shape at different excitation light are almost similar, while the spectral intensity differs substantially. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the shape and intensity of the spectra become more shaper and higher as excitation light enhances from 290 nm to 340 nm. We assumed that the absorption of each component of the colloid increases with excitation light of 340 nm, as that the excitation light of 340 nm affects almost all luminescence centers in the lattices. This result was compliant with the observations by Razbirin et al., [20] that stated the possibility of emission at certain wavelength depends on the arrangement of molecule complexity of emitting centers. It can be implicit that CDs-amine modified display the maximum PL emission with the excitation at 340 nm which is dependent of excitation. According to Kharangarh et al., [21] this implies the uniformity of particle size distribution of CDs before and after modification with amine-modified oligos was preserved. It has been shown in Fig. 6 that CDs-amine modified have a PL peak at approximately 450 nm with the excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 340 nm and the observed PL peak exhibited differently in the optimum emission intensity (a.u.) under different excitation light. By using different excitation light, Dresselhaus et al., [22] demonstrated that localization of electron hole pair clusters of isolated sp2and size effect could provide clues to the shifting of PL peak. As the excitation wavelength changed from 290 nm to 340 nm in the CDs-amine modified, the PL peak remained at wavelength of 450 nm indicating uniform particle size distribution of CDs. Excitation wavelengths of 320 nm, 330 nm and 350 nm were not included in the results presented in Fig. 6 due to the overflow reading of fluorescence intensity of the CDs emission spectra at several scanned wavelengths. Therefore, only the complete readings of fluorescence intensity of the CDs emission spectra were reported and excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 6a) was chosen for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectra overlap between fluorophore and quencher
ce:section.ce:para: Not all fluorescence quenching require spectral overlap and close proximity between the energy fluorophore and quencher. However, for fluorescence modulation in macromolecules studies, sensitivity to small changes in distance between fluorophore and quencher is essential. In fact, fluorescence quenching has long been considered as a spectroscopy ruler for the design of fluorescent sensors. By providing the good spectral overlap between the fluorophore and quencher ensure it feasibility [23].

As shown in Fig. 7 , an excellent spectral overlap is revealed between the donor emission of CDs and the acceptor absorption of AuNPs. This notable overlap designates the credible photon less energy transfer between the CDs and AuNPs in fluorescence quenching system. At optimized concentration ratio of the donor–acceptor components, an outstanding fluorescence quenching peak at 450 nm is noticed (Fig. 7). Therefore, the PL peak at 450 nm could be credited to the CDs emission when excited at CDs absorption maximum at 340 nm. Again, the good spectral overlap can be measured as the additional factor for efficient fluorescence quenching signal as proven in Fig. 7 [23].

To improve further the efficiency of fluorescence quenching system, the effect of reaction time has been evaluated. Fig. 8 shows the typical quenching results of CDs-AuNPs which involved two steps. First, the CDs-amine modified (5.7 μM) at specific time interval (1, 1.5 and 2 hrs) in the presence of the target oligos and second, by addition of the AuNPs-thiol modified at varied time interval (30 min, 1 h, 1.5 h and 2 h). As shown in Fig. 8, incubation time of CDs-amine modified with target oligos for 1 and 1.5 h, followed incubation with AuNPs-thiol modified for 30 min, 1 h, 1.5 h and 2 h, the net fluorescence quenching (F0-Fq) of CDs was decrease when the time increase. For incubation time of CDs-amine modified with target oligos for 2 h and followed incubation with AuNPs-thiol modified for 30 min, the highest net fluorescence quenching (F0-Fq) of CDs was observed. Above 30 min incubation with AuNPs-thiol modified, the net fluorescence quenching was approximately constant indicating that the reaction had reached saturation. This observation suggests that the sequence of CDs-amine modified is fully hybridized by the target oligos and then co-hybridized with AuNPs-thiol modified stabilizing the effect of quenching on CDs. On the other hand, the increase in contact time will not led to a significant increase in the number of AuNPs-thiol modified that come into close proximity with the surface of CDs. Lin et al. [24] and Hai et al. [25] have reported similar observation. The target oligos as an intermediate that encourage the formation of satellite structure of CDs-AuNPs through co-hybridization have reached the optimum response after 2 h and 30 min of incubation time. At this stage, the increase of incubation time does not improve the efficiency of quenching. Therefore, the incubation time of 2 h and 30 min for co-hybridization of target oligos with CDs-amine modified and AuNPs-thiol modified was selected for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the quenching system
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum condition, the quenching system is used for the detection of target gene in various concentrations from 0.01 nM to 200 nM. Subsequent co-hybridization of target oligos with reporter (on CDs) and quencher probe (on AuNPs) it will increase the numbers of the absorption spectrum overlap with fluorescence spectrum, triggering the increasing in the CDs fluorescence quenching. The phenomenon was due to the outstanding quencher’s characteristics of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The transfer of photon-deficient energy from the fluorophore to the quencher and the molar extinction coefficients of the plasmon AuNPs band were more efficient and higher with the enrichment of target oligos co-hybridization. The fluorescent intensity of the fluorophore is quenched following the adding of target oligos that expands the capability to quench the fluorophore fluorescence. Therefore, evaluating the fluorescence intensity transforms of CDs the fliC gene of E. coli O157:H7 could be verified in this study. Fig. 9 A depicted the behavior of fluorescence spectra of CDs when exposed to the target oligos at different concentrations. It shows that the fluorescence intensity of CDs slowly declined with increasing concentration of the target oligos. The results show that in the range of concentrations studied, CDs is expected to exhibit static quenching behavior of Stern-Volmer quenching reaction. There is no shift in the maximum emission wavelength observed, even for the high concentration of target oligos [26]. Hence, the presence of target oligos in the solution retains the static quenching sensory while suppressing dynamic quenching in the system.

Fig. 9B shows a linear correlation between the quenched fluorescence intensity of CDs and the logarithm concentration of target oligos in the concentration range of 0.01–100 nM. Above target oligo concentration of 100 nM, the CDs-AuNPs system reached saturation point. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be at 1.03 ± 3.54 nM (n = 3), with the linear regression of y = 675.6 log x + 11739 (R2 = 0.992). The equation of 3Sa/b was used to calculate the LOD which standard deviation of the blank and the slope of the calibration plot is denoted by Sa and b, respectively with the ratio of signal-to-noise is equal to 3 [27]. The present findings for LOD is comparable with the previous work reported by Shi et al., [28], Tao et al., [29], Mao et al., [30], Wang et al., [31] and Bokhari et al., [32] for the construction of fluorescence DNA-based sensor (Table 1 ) which is in the range of 1.0–3.5 nM.

The efficiency of fluorescence quenching was calculated based on equation of
∑ = 1 -
F
0
-
F
q
F
D
. FD is the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore without the quencher, while F0 and Fq are the fluorescence intensities of CDs-amine modified at 0 and 30 min of quenching with the quencher (with 340 nm/450 nm of excitation/emission peaks), respectively. The target oligos net quenching efficiency was ranged from 1.38% to 4.93% (Table 2 ).

Generally, fluorescence quenching is every process that involves decreasing in the fluorescence intensity of a molecule. Formation a complex between the fluorophore and quencher in the ground-state is involved in the static quenching. Namely, it is absorption of the light formed by fluorescence excitation of the donor fluorophore to proximate quencher chromophore. Without emission of a photon the complex immediately returns to the ground state and quench. To specify the mechanism either follows an inner filter effect, static quenching or dynamic quenching, the changes in absorption spectrum of CDs was assessed. As showed in Fig. 9C, the absorption spectrum of CDs-oligos had varies from CDs absorption spectrum [33]. Thus, eliminate the system from follow the dynamic quenching. It is anticipated that fluorescence quenching of the fluorometric assay was via the creation of nonfluorescent complex between intact fluorophore and quencher in ground-state equilibrium which suggests static quenching mechanism. Given the fact that there is no overlapping between absorption spectrum of oligonucleotides and the excitation spectrum of CDs [34], (Fig. 9D), the mechanism of inner filter effect can also be eliminated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of the CDs-AuNPs system
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison purposes, the system is tested on different oligonucleotide sequences and bacterial strain to determine the selectivity and specificity as depicted in Fig. 10 a and b. The length of sequence and concentration of all target oligos were fixed at 20 bp and 100 nM for evaluation in the selectivity study. The differences between the one-base and two-base mismatches target oligos compared to the complementary target oligos are one and two base incompatible in the oligonucleotide sequence, respectively. Relatively the fluorescence intensity changes were lower for the two-base mismatch (74.54 ± 7.13%) than the one-base mismatch (76.77 ± 2.83%) and complementary (91.61 ± 1.71%). The results of the selectivity study show that the system is selective even for only one-base mismatch in a sequence of target oligos.

The system was also evaluated towards different bacterial strain other than E. coli O157:H7. All the bacteria genome was fixed at 1.7 × 105 copy number for the specificity study. The change in fluorescence intensity of E. coli O157:H7, Salmonella enteritidis and Listeria monocytogenes were 93.49 ± 1.15%, 80.95 ± 5.69% and 84.16 ± 3.38%, respectively. The specificity study indicates that the system is specific and feasible even when a very long sequence (genomic DNA) of target oligos is used. This results indicate that inadequate base pairs and different bacterial strain have less co-hybridization event with the CDs-amine and AuNPs-thiol. Features of the current fluorometric assay are comparable with others reported by Shi et al., [28] and Sun et al., [35]. Bacterial genomic DNA is used in specificity study without amplification for short sequences and the results showed that it can be distinguished. By using the genomic DNA and bypassing the DNA amplification step by PCR, it reduces the time of sample preparation. Additionally, it is not practical to amplify bacterial genomic DNA from other bacterial strains by PCR, as the specificity of the PCR primers, the genomic DNA will not be amplified. Therefore, when tested in the system, other bacteria strain will resemble the sample without a target. The results of using bacterial genomic DNA in a specificity study prove that static quenching is independent of distance (the length of sequences of target oligos). Gold reveal efficient quenching of the fluorescence of fluorophore in the energy transfer system in the distance range of 15–60 nm. The sensitivity of this CDs-AuNPs system was shown not depending on the distance between the fluorophore and quencher. Thus, it is differentiate static quenching from another categories of energy transfer, nanometal surface energy transfer (NSET) that follows a short distance [36]. It is noticeable that the current method offers good selectivity and specificity towards different oligonucleotide sequences and bacterial strain.

For real samples analysis the samples were analyzed against real time PCR method. A linear correlation (y = 59.45x + 598.8) between net fluorescence quenching and amplicon concentration with R2 = 0.964 and LOD is 1.78 ± 3.54 ng µL−1 (n = 3) was obtained (Fig. 11 ). Data for a series of fliC gene (amplicon) concentration was listed in Table 3 . As summarized in Table 4 , the detection of fliC gene using real time PCR methods was in the range of 0.045–41.90 ng µL−1 while the fluorometric assay gave the concentration in the range of 0.62–31.99 ng µL−1. Statistical analysis for comparison between the means of the fluorometric assay and real time PCR method was also conducted. It is noteworthy that the calculated t-value is less than the critical t-value (tcalc. < 2.78) indicating that the developed method and real time PCR method are comparable and in good agreement. Detail on calculation of real samples analysis for fluorometric assay was documented in Table 5 .

The DNA-based assay was developed instead of immuno-based assay due to several advantages such as (i) unnecessary of in-vivo immunization, (ii) binding of target is rich in reproducible structures, (iii) thermal and chemical stability are higher, (iv) electrochemical and optical detection are easy to achieve and (v) binding sites of some targets can be found to be more than one [37]. Nucleic acid was a preferred target of detection as synthesis of DNA probes is cost effective and less time consuming than antibodies that rely on costly animal hosts to produce antibodies which usually take longer time. Another reason, DNA probes are easily conjugated and modified on reporter/labeled molecules than antibodies, it makes DNA biosensors more flexible than immunosensors. The small size of the nucleic acid from the antibody compensates for the efficiency of the biosensor that enables high-density of DNA probe monolayers [38].

The recent methods for quantification of E. coli gene include real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [39], electrochemical [40], and quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) biosensor [41]. Although real-time PCR has good specificity and high sensitivity for E. coli gene quantification with limit of detection (LOD) in the fg amount, it requires a multiplex sample preparation procedures. DNA extraction and purification are required to prevent sample impurities that will inhibit the amplification [39]. Although real time PCR based methods are high throughput analysis, it involves costly reagents and the instrument should be handled by trained personnel.

The fluorometric assay are less costly and not complex in sample preparation which can be done by the first timer user as it involve less tedious procedure to quantify the concentrations upper than 1 nM DNA target of E. coli O157:H7. Contrarily, electrochemical biosensors for the quantification of E. coli O157:H7, the developed sensors are very sensitive to non-specific recognition which resulting in the lack of specificity. Consequently, current research in the enhancement of electrochemical biosensors needs to focus on the improvement of sample collections and preparations for effective electron transduction [40]. Compared with the fluorometric assay the complexity and viscosity of the food matrices have no effect on the performance and it is capable to quantify E. coli O157:H7 with good specificity. Contrary to QCM DNA sensors that have been encountered difficulties while studying surface deposition of nanoparticles that is not prepared in the readily form of sensor surface [42].

A fluorescence quenching energy transfer as ‘spectroscopic rulers’ between CDs and AuNPs has been demonstrated for the determination of E. coli O157:H7 fliC gene. Following the target oligos co-hybridization, the fluorescence intensity of CDs quenched slightly and the quenching efficiency of the fluorescence of the CDs with target oligos is greater than that of CDs without target oligos. The results showed the effectiveness of this fluorescence quenching mechanism for the detection of bacteria gene. This method indicates the fliC gene of E. coli O157:H7 could be traced based on the fluorescence signal variations of CDs in the fluorescence quenching. This proved that this method has a potential to be utilized for the verification of selectivity and specificity towards different oligonucleotides sequence and bacteria strain.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This study was jointly sponsored by University Putra Malaysia and MARDI under Putra Grant (GP-IPB/2016/9515403) and Development Grant (P-RI-401). Authors also acknowledge the Jabatan Perkhidmatan Awam, Malaysia for scholarship under the Hadiah Latihan Persekutuan.
This work was funded by the University Putra Malaysia, Malaysia and MARDI through Putra Grant (GP-IPB/2016/9515403) and Development Grant (P-RI-401).
This article does not contain information from any studies that were carried out with human or animals.
No informed consent was obtained as human participants were not involved in this work.
